personas.name: Budget-Conscious Support Seeker
personas.description: A user who wants mental health support tools but needs clear premium value before paying for access.
goals: Continue mood tracking without unexpected costs
Understand which features are free and which require premium
Pay only when premium benefits are clear
pain_points: Core features feel locked after a short free experience
Subscription value is unclear before purchase
Paid upgrades can feel risky when money is limited
context: Trying the app for self-help or early therapy support
Comparing MindDoc with lower-cost or free alternatives
constraints: Needs transparent premium information before purchase
personas.notes: Refined the automated persona by replacing generic mental-health language with grounded affordability and paywall concerns from the revised review group.

personas.name: Structured Check-In User
personas.description: A user who depends on reminders and routine-based check-ins to maintain a consistent mood-tracking habit.
goals: Complete morning, afternoon, and evening check-ins consistently
Recover missed check-ins without losing reflections
pain_points: Notifications are missing, too frequent, or easy to miss
Fixed check-in timing does not fit the user's schedule
Entries can be lost when a time window closes
context: Balancing the app with work, school, or irregular routines
Using short check-ins instead of long journaling sessions
constraints: Reminder times must fit the user's routine
Missed check-ins should not discard the user's input
personas.notes: Refined the automated persona by grounding it in reminder timing, notification reliability, and flexible check-in behavior rather than broad positive tracking language.

personas.name: Reflective Mood Tracker
personas.description: A user who records moods frequently and wants a clear history of entries to reflect on emotional patterns.
pain_points: It is hard to maintain a consistent mood log without app support
Past entries can be difficult to review in one place
Tracking feels incomplete when mood history is hard to interpret
context: Using the app as a diary-like reflection tool
constraints: Entries need to be stored with usable history
The history view must support reflection on past moods
personas.notes: Refined the automated persona by focusing on diary-like mood logging, entry history, and reflection instead of unsupported report-sharing behavior.

personas.name: Emotional Reflection User
personas.description: A user who uses the app to put feelings into words and values emotionally supportive reflection content.
goals: Use supportive exercises, insights, or readings to reflect
pain_points: It can be difficult to describe emotions clearly
Emotional reflection feels less useful when support content is vague
The user wants check-ins to feel personally meaningful
context: Using the app during stressful or emotional periods
Returning to readings, exercises, and check-ins for self-reflection
constraints: The app must support written emotional reflection
Support content must be available after the user records feelings
personas.notes: Refined the automated persona by grounding it in emotional reflection, feeling understood, and supportive insight content instead of unsupported privacy and continuity assumptions.

personas.name: Therapy-Support User
personas.description: A user who uses MindDoc to understand patterns, review mental-health summaries, and support conversations with professional care.
goals: Access reports or summaries that support self-understanding
Use app resources or guidance alongside therapy or self-help
pain_points: Reports and insights can feel too broad or too expensive to access
Guidance can become less useful when tied too heavily to paid services
Mental-health resources are harder to use when they are not clearly connected to tracked patterns
context: Using the app for ongoing self-monitoring across weeks
Bringing app-generated information into therapy or doctor discussions
constraints: Progress summaries must be understandable outside the moment of check-in
Support resources must remain available without requiring outside promotional services
personas.notes: Refined the automated persona by grounding it in reports, mental-health summaries, resources, and support for therapy conversations instead of expressive-label customization.
